transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.

Pretty good, man.

Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
That's exciting.

I on Friday or Saturday.

don't have any.

But we'd love to just kind of like hear in your own words, you know, just a quick run through of your sales experience, why you've made some transitions, and then what interested you in growthx in particular.
Yeah, awesome.
So really kicked off my career close to 10 years ago, and primarily a lot of my experience has been in the agency world.
So I've worked with a software development agency, and I was kind of like the founding BDR and AE in that role with another buddy of mine.
And we grew that company from basically like 14 reps to like 30.
30 to 40 employee size, and really the growth was primarily directed on the direct correlation between me and my counterpart, which is a really exciting time.

You really get to build out the whole sales process, build the collateral, did a lot of hunting myself, and then we collaborated with a lot of the solutions engineers, CEO, CTO, and it was pretty well.
Some of that's not technical at all.
I had to navigate a lot of that was like a huge accelerator in my career.

That then just kind of like pushed me into the tech scene pretty heavily because we were kind of like the partner to work with when you had, you know, sort of app projects or web projects that you want to accelerate on and hit timelines.
Our team would come in and be like a, just an extension of your dev team and your product team.
So that was Quantum Mob for a few years, close to three.
And then I did some product.
Which is, it's really the same company, Hotjar, and that was my first time in a SaaS world.
So it got a bit more of like a, you your conventional SaaS experience sales cycle, which was, at the time, Hotjar was like heavy, heavy product market fit, aggressive inbound.
We were probably getting like, yeah, close to like 10 to 20 like calls per week or like set meetings.
So we were managing that for quite a while.
then, and that was more of like an SMB, higher mid-market type opportunities, dealing with a lot of marketing folk, a lot of product and data folk.

And, and yeah, that's more mid-market and, and enterprise deals right now.

and then, yeah, so like, you know, I was, I was telling Tucker this, like, I'm pretty comfortable with where I'm at right now.

I thought that made complete sense.

I'm like, okay, fine.

I just wanted to.

the company ended up shutting down basically.

you you.
ContentSquare, really.

There's a real need of analytics and behavioral activity on their site or on their apps.

So if they have like a GA or another analytics tool where they're just getting figures and numbers, Hotjar really helps to provide the full picture of like the how and the why.

So for them, when it comes back to like reporting or positioning a statement internally with the CMO or making initiative on their roadmap, they can now bring some justification into that change and say, hey, we know we're.

I don't know why I did that.

positive.
for you.

What are, like, the main metrics or outcomes that you connect ContentSquare to the client's growth strategy for?

are
For shortened flows.

If I'm working with like marketing folk, whether they're like in digital marketing or demand gen.
Much holistically, but like campaign specific.
Yeah, that is important, like context.
I think what's really important is that we prioritize kind of like on a wide funnel, you know, what is happening within C-Suite that contributes to this campaign.

I feel like that's, what's going to really help us have more value in what we, how we position ourselves.
So, so yes, yes.
And, and a bit more in terms of like what the business is trying to achieve.
I love that.
Yeah, definitely.
It's like, you know, ask why a couple more times than you want to.

Yeah.

And then eventually you.
Don't get executive alignment.
Like, hey, can you bring in your CMO?
This is a pretty hefty investment.

For sure.
For sure.
Taking me back to some of your outbound days, and I know you've done some of that currently.

One that still works pretty well.
So back in pre-COVID, LinkedIn was like a machine for meetings.
Like, I would hit up like C-suite and VPs and be like, hey, can just pick your brain on?

Everyone would want to talk about it.
And we'd say, hey, let me just grab a coffee and pick your brain on digital transformation.
So at the time that really worked.
And I kind of pivoted towards like, hey, I'm going to be, I know, downtown Toronto.
I'm meeting with certain clients.
I'd love to just like grab a coffee and just connect.
On your data strategy or your product analytics strategy, keeping it super high level and just trying to like, hey, I'm in a certain location for a certain period of time.
I tend to get better responses and some meetings.
And that's only when I try to leverage my location as like the main factor.
But I have to say, man, Outbound has been really tough lately.
Absolutely.
That is a rough world to be in right now with a ton of tools and a ton of noise.
But that's why I try to make it a bit more super specific to location and a light commitment of a coffee, which hopefully people are still open to.
But that works fairly well, even now.
Yeah, totally.
It's got to be something that cuts through the noise.
It's got to be something that's relevant to like where they're at or where, you know, your solution like meets what they could be looking for or even like what the data is telling.
I feel like.
Leveraging intent on channels that aren't, you know, phone is unbelievably important, even with phone.
I mean, just out of curiosity, like what channel do you feel like is the best for Outbound today?

This was like a $650,000 deal.

And we were kind of entertaining it.
And then at some point in time, I reached out to other members within that org.
So it began to like multi-thread it a bit more.
And then from that contact, this person named Karen, and I was working with another guy named Claude, who was initial inbound.
Like me and my founder, like we're...
We're really excited about this account, so we want to dig into what else is happening at the company.
Because typically when an account expresses that they want to be able to invest in development hours and have more devs, they're trying to build something.
They're trying to get something off the ground.
So I spoke with the VP who was really managing like cards and payments, found out that there was a massive redesign project that opened up.
And her counterpart was looking into developers that built it out.
So we like piece these two separate projects that were ultimately in collaboration.
And we kind of CC them in an email.
Like, hey, we got connected.
We were learning about these two things.
We have developers and we have like super senior designers.
Could we run like a workshop and another discovery of how we can like build this out together?
You know, obviously you can imagine back and forth, a number of, you know, a number of like trials and proof of concepts that we had to build out.
and forth.

So many different meetings, working with designers.

Um, and then they want to redesign that app entirely.

This is what we can do.
Imagine on a full scale.

Yeah.

Nice.

was kind of following up with all the contacts, ensuring deadlines were being met, or at least we know the timelines that we're working towards.

Okay.
Okay.
I think that was like the biggest part.
Nice.

And then also just coming in there a more confident.
You know what mean?
Like basically a BDR, basically kind of like a hybrid AE, just running in there like, hey, awesome ideas.
it just kind of like a headless chicken pulling on everyone to get work done.

Definitely.
I can relate.
We're building the plan as we fly it here.

I just want to get a sense of it.

Like they had nothing.

Like inbound leads, whenever there's a second person selling services, and that's going to happen like ASAP, right?
Like Nigel would be involved in some of that.

With the visibility and monitoring, it's like no competitors charging more than 20K.
